title: AdaGrad & RMSprop: Adaptive Step Sizes
concept: Adaptive algorithms scale learning rates per parameter, giving rare features larger steps and frequent ones smaller, fine-tuned steps.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main purpose of adding Momentum to gradient descent?

options.text: To smooth out noisy side-to-side oscillations and roll over local minima.
To increase the number of training rows.

exercises.explain: Correct! Momentum uses past steps to guide the current one. Now, how do we adjust steps when some weights update much less than others?

cards.title: Sparse signals
cards.body: In a text model, common words like "the" appear on every page. Rare words like "hyperdrive" appear once in a million reviews. Should we update their weights at the same speed?

cards.title: Adaptive Learning Rates
cards.body: Algorithms like AdaGrad and RMSprop scale the step size for each weight individually based on how active that weight has been.

cards.title: The search party
cards.body: If a scout path is highly traveled (frequent updates), take small, cautious steps (small learning rate). If a path is rarely explored (rare updates), take a giant leap to investigate.

cards.title: AdaGrad vs. RMSprop
cards.body: AdaGrad shrinks steps based on the sum of all historical updates, but it can shrink them to zero too fast. RMSprop fixes this by focusing only on recent updates.

cards.title: Not one global rate
cards.body: Most beginners think there is only one 'learning rate' for the whole model. In adaptive optimizers like AdaGrad and RMSprop, every single parameter adjusts its own learning speed dynamically!

cards.title: Standard vs. Adaptive
cards.body: Comparing how parameters learn under different optimization methods:

left.text:
One rate fits all weights
Sparse features learn slowly

right.text:
Per-weight custom rates
Rare features get large steps

exercises.prompt: Why do we want adaptive learning rates for sparse features (features that rarely show up)?

options.text: Because they need larger steps to make meaningful progress when they do appear.
Because rare features should be ignored by the model.
To make the training run slower.
To keep the model's weights positive.

exercises.explain: Correct! Rare features update infrequently. When they do appear, adaptive rates scale up their step size so the model actually learns from them.

exercises.prompt: True or false: RMSprop improves on AdaGrad by scaling steps using only a moving average of recent updates instead of the entire history.
exercises.explain: True! AdaGrad accumulates all history, which can make learning rates shrink to zero too early. RMSprop fixes this by decaying the historical memory.

exercises.sentence: Adaptive optimizers dynamically scale the step size for each ___ individually.
exercises.explain: Yes! Individual parameters (weights) get custom learning rates adjusted on the fly based on their update history.

exercises.prompt: What is a drawback of the basic AdaGrad algorithm?

options.text: The learning rate can shrink too quickly and prevent the model from learning further.
It makes the learning rate grow to infinity.
It only works on small datasets.
It is only used for image data.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Since AdaGrad continually adds up all past gradients, the denominator grows infinitely, forcing steps to stall. RMSprop resolves this!

exercises.prompt: You are building a text recommendation engine. Words like 'pneumatics' are rare but crucial when they occur. A standard optimizer is learning nothing from them. Which optimizer is best suited to fix this?

options.text: RMSprop (or another adaptive learning rate optimizer)
Standard Batch Gradient Descent with a fixed learning rate
A non-adaptive optimizer with momentum set to zero
Stochastic Gradient Descent without any learning rate

exercises.explain: Fabulous! Adaptive optimizers automatically scale up learning rates for infrequently updated features, ensuring sparse signals are not lost. You've conquered the fundamentals of gradient tuning!
